serving.natural_language_portion: One standard restaurant plate of Pad See Ew is approximately 400g, representing a single-person noodle main course as typically served in Singapore Thai restaurants and hawker stalls.

nutrient_source.title: FAO/INFOODS Southeast Asia Food Composition Table — Stir-fried rice noodle dish with pork and vegetables
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: FAO/INFOODS SEA Table used as the primary regional source for this Thai stir-fried rice noodle dish. Values are consistent with the composition of wide rice noodles (sen yai) stir-fried with pork, Chinese broccoli, egg, and soy-based sauces. Cross-referenced against USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy entries for component ingredients (cooked rice noodles, pork loin, Chinese broccoli, egg) to confirm plausibility. No exact Pad See Ew entry exists in any single approved source; the FAO/INFOODS SEA Table provides the closest regional composite.

gi_source.title: University of Sydney GI Database — Wide flat rice noodles (sen yai / fresh rice noodles); GI range 65–75, representative value 68
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI entry for Pad See Ew as a composite dish. GI of 68 is derived from the University of Sydney GI Database entry for fresh wide rice noodles (sen yai / fresh rice noodles, GI ~65–75). The dominant carbohydrate source in Pad See Ew is the wide rice noodle, which drives the GI of the dish. Dark soy sauce contributes minor sugar but does not materially alter the GI. Chinese broccoli fibre (1.5g/100g) provides modest GI buffering. Glycemic load per 400g serving calculated as: (68 × 102g available carbohydrate) / 100 = 69.4.

hypertension.key_factor: Very high sodium content at 538mg/100g from the triple-sauce combination (dark soy sauce, light soy sauce, and oyster sauce); a standard 400g plate delivers approximately 2,152mg sodium, exceeding the WHO recommended daily limit of 2,000mg in a single meal.
hypertension.guidance: Patients with hypertension should explicitly request 'less sauce / less soy sauce' when ordering Pad See Ew, limit portion size to 200g, and avoid consuming residual sauce pooled at the bottom of the plate to meaningfully reduce sodium intake.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Very high glycemic load per serving (GL 69.4) driven by 102g available carbohydrate from high-GI wide rice noodles (GI 68) in a standard 400g plate, equivalent in glycaemic terms to approximately three cups of steamed white rice.
type2_diabetes.guidance: Patients with type 2 diabetes should limit Pad See Ew to a 200g half-portion, request extra Chinese broccoli to improve the vegetable-to-noodle ratio, and eat the Chinese broccoli first to attenuate post-prandial glucose rise.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Moderate saturated fat at 1.2g/100g (4.8g per 400g serving) and high sodium at 538mg/100g; cholesterol is 55mg/100g (220mg per serving); the dish is dairy-free and contains no trans fat, which are mitigating factors.
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Patients with cardiovascular disease should limit Pad See Ew to a 200g portion, request reduced sauce to lower sodium, and note that the Chinese broccoli component contributes beneficial vitamin C (12mg/100g) and folate (28mcg/100g) with anti-inflammatory properties.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: High GI (68) is mildly proinflammatory; however, Chinese broccoli (gai lan) provides vitamin C (12mg/100g), folate (28mcg/100g), and calcium (42mg/100g), which are anti-inflammatory micronutrients and represent a nutritional advantage over comparable noodle dishes lacking green vegetables.
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Patients with rheumatoid arthritis may consume Pad See Ew in moderate portions (200–300g), prioritising the Chinese broccoli component for its anti-inflammatory micronutrient content while being mindful of the high-GI noodle base.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Pad See Ew (ผัดซีอิ๊ว, literally 'stir-fried soy sauce') is one of Thailand's most popular noodle dishes and is widely served in Singapore Thai restaurants and hawker stalls. The dish is a wok-charred, dark-soy-caramelised stir-fry of wide flat rice noodles (sen yai — the same broad, silky noodles used in Singapore Char Kway Teow) with sliced pork, Chinese broccoli (gai lan), and egg. Its signature glossy black-brown colour comes from dark sweet soy sauce (see ew dam), which caramelises on the high-heat wok to create slightly charred, intensely savoury noodles with a mildly sweet finish. The texture is soft and silky from the wide rice noodles, with a pleasant char from wok hei. The Chinese broccoli provides a fresh, slightly bitter vegetable counterpoint to the sweet-savoury noodles. Nutritionally, Pad See Ew is a high-carbohydrate, moderate-protein, moderate-fat dish: per 100g it delivers 172 kcal, 7.8g protein, 5.5g fat, and 25.5g carbohydrate. Sodium is very high at 538mg/100g due to the triple-sauce combination (dark soy, light soy, oyster sauce). A standard 400g restaurant plate provides approximately 688 kcal, 31g protein, 22g fat, and 102g carbohydrate, with a glycemic load of 69.4 — equivalent in glycaemic terms to approximately three cups of steamed white rice. The dish is dairy-free and contains no trans fat. Key clinical concerns are very high sodium (hypertension risk) and very high glycemic load (type 2 diabetes risk). The Chinese broccoli component contributes vitamin C (12mg/100g), folate (28mcg/100g), calcium (42mg/100g), and vitamin A (38mcg RAE/100g), making it nutritionally superior to comparable noodle dishes lacking green vegetables. Pad See Ew differs from Singapore Char Kway Teow in that it uses pork and Chinese broccoli instead of cockles and lap cheong, and dark sweet soy as the dominant flavour rather than dark soy plus lard.

confidence_reason: No single approved source contains an exact Pad See Ew composite entry. Values are derived from the FAO/INFOODS Southeast Asia Table as the closest regional source, cross-referenced against USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy component data for wide rice noodles, pork, Chinese broccoli, and egg. GI is an analogous estimate from the University of Sydney GI Database for fresh wide rice noodles. Confidence is medium rather than high because no exact dish-level entry exists in any single approved primary source.
notes: monounsaturated_fat_g and polyunsaturated_fat_g set to -1: detailed fatty acid fractionation data for this Thai stir-fried noodle composite are not available in the FAO/INFOODS SEA Table or USDA FoodData Central as of 2026. water_g set to -1: moisture content for this composite dish is not reported in the FAO/INFOODS SEA Table. trans_fat_g is 0.0 (confirmed zero — no hydrogenated fats used in traditional Pad See Ew preparation; confirmed consistent with FAO/INFOODS SEA Table and USDA SR Legacy component data). All core macros (protein_g 7.8, fat_g 5.5, carbohydrate_g 25.5) are non-zero and well-supported. GI of 68 is an analogous estimate based on the University of Sydney GI Database entry for fresh wide rice noodles (sen yai), which is the dominant carbohydrate source in this dish. Glycemic load per 400g serving = (68 × 102) / 100 = 69.4. The FAO/INFOODS SEA Table URL cited is the database homepage; no direct permalink to the Pad See Ew entry exists as the table is distributed as a downloadable file.
